Data, Information &
Knowledge

Defining and Converting

What   i s  t he  di ffer ence  bet ween  dat a  and  i nfor mat i on?


Data  is raw characters, numbers, or symbols
In the digital era, data is fundamentally 0s and 1s, or
sequences of 0s and 1s, that are stored together and
can be transferred around a network                                                                     Data
Data that isn’t processed or organized not really useful                                 Order


Information is data that is organized in some way
Maybe units are added to the data                                                                 Context
Maybe similar types of data are organized in table
Information systems are fundamentally about storing
and presenting data in an efficient and useful way

                                                                                          Information

What   do  we  mean  by  dat a  qual i t y?


                      Completeness: Are any values missing?


                Val i d i ty: Do the entered values make sense?


      Uniqueness: Is each transaction, entity, occurrence re p re s e nt e d   onl y   once ?



      Consistency: Does summary information align with
                                             detailed information?


        Timeliness: Is the data current with respect to the
                                          business requirements?
                                                                                          Note: These can be applied to a single field,
                                                                                               a whole table, or a whole database
                                Accuracy: Are all values correct?





https://www.amurta.com/data-quality/

What   ar e  Dat abases?  How  do  t hey  hel p?








                                                                                                             Data re d u n d a n c yand
                                                                                                             data inconsistency are

                                                                                                             be shared among all the
                                                                                                             users of the data.























(Adapted from  Turban & Volonino2011, TG3-2)
                                                                                                                                             14

What  arerelational                    databases work?


A relational database is a collection of data items
organized as a set of formally-described tables
They describe entities    and their attributes e.g., person
and eye colour


They describe the re l at i ons hi pbetween entities too
e.g., person and house


Data can be accessed or reassembled in many
different ways without having to reorganize the
database tables.










                                                                                                                                   15

         What   ar e  t he  di ffer ent   t ypes  of  dat abase?

What   ar e  ent i t i es,   at t r i but es,   and  r ecor ds?

transaction or event about which
information is stored                                        Student_Table

data elements

How do we create rel ati onshi ps?

table. Cannot be blank or null (no value)

re l at i ons hi p   ( l i nk)   b e t w e e n  t he   t w o  t ab l e s .   Mus t   ex i s t   i n  t he   ot he r   t ab l e   or   b e   nul l

DBMS allows users to create, read, update and delete data in a relational database

What   i s  dat a  r edundancy?


The same data is present in more than one table


For example
Jones, 48, Male, Teacher
Jones, 48, 3 Advent Drive
Jones, employee number 22345


Positive: a positive type of data redundancy works to safeguard data and promote consistency.


Negative: Where not intentional, data redundancy can lead to big problems with data
inconsistency, where one update does not automatically update another field.

What   ar e  i nt egr i t y  const r ai nt s?


Integrity constraints are the rules that help ensure the quality of information


Relational integrity constraint
An order cannot be shipped without an address


Business-critical integrity constraint
Product returns are not accepted for fresh produce 15 days after purchase
A discount maximum of 20 per cent may be given.


















                                                                                                                                        23

What   ar e  l ogi cal   and  physi cal   vi ews?


Views provide users of the database with different
views


There is only one physical view i.e. the physical
storage of information on a storage device


Can be multiple logical views i.e. how individual
users logically access information to meet their
ow n   p a r t i c u l a r   b u s i n e s s   n e e d s

How do databases ensure securi ty?


Password: provides authentication of the
user


Access level: determines who has access
to the different types of information


Access control: determines types of user
access, such as read-only access


This is at the basic user level. Database
security is a whole topic in itself and
forms part of a larger IT risk and security
plan

What   ar e  scal abi l i t y  and  per for mance?


Remember from last week?


A database must scale to meet increased demand, while
maintaining acceptable performance levels


Scalability: how well a system can adapt to increased
demands?


Performance: how quickly a system performs a certain
process or transaction















                                                                                                                                26

Business Intelligence
and Analytics
What? Why? How?

What   i s  anal yt i cal   i nfor mat i on?


In Information Systems we distinguish analytical
information from transactional                       information


Tr  a   n   s   a   c   t   i   o   n   a   linformation are the data and artefacts we
use for administering our day-to-day business activities
e.g., individual sales (customer + product + amount)


Analytical information is the aggregation or summary
statistics of our transactional data e.g., monthly sales


To           r     u    n          a           b     u    s     i     n    e     s     s           w     e     l     l     ,           w     e           n    e     e     d      both –but, database
technologies were traditionally geared more towards
just capturing the transactional












https://sites.google.com/site/qcckclark/06-valuing-organizational-inform ation

What   was  t he  ‘ bi g  dat a’   r evol ut i on?


Most business are now data rich, but, information poor


Over the last two decades it got much easier and cheaper to capture data and store it (e.g., think
Amazon web services)


Businesses have access to masses of data i.e., mobile devices, sensors, wearables, Internet-of-
Things, social media, digital images, emails


That all good, but, the challenge is to convert the data into useful and easy to understand
information for managers


This has ended up being no small task!










                                                                                                                                          29

What   i s  busi ness  I nt el l i gence?


“Information that results from the processing of operational data to create
information that exposes patterns, relationships and trends of importance to the
organisation” …. like uncovering new product demands


“Information collected from multiple sources such as suppliers, customers,
competitors, partners and industries that analyses patterns, trends and relationships
for strategic decision making”


Business intelligence is an umbrella term for the people, tools, processes, and
outcomes that seek to enhance the analytical side of data usage in business


It has now become a huge industry in itself!


Aside from advertising, the compilation and ‘selling’ of business data is at the core of
Big Tech business models. Highly controversial!





(Kroenke 2014, p. 602).
(Baltzan, Lynch & Fisher 2015, p. 14)                                                                                                            30

How does busi ness i ntel l i gence work?

























































Adapted from Rainer & Cegielski2012
                                                                                                                                 31

What   ar e  OLAP  and  OLTP?

Data is data (0s and1s), and databases are databases, however, early on it was recognized that
analytical database re q ui re me nt s   w e re   d i f f e re nt  to transactional database requirements


The general rule is to keep transactional DBs separate from analytical DBs.                         Otherwise –
performance issues! i.e., complex analytical queries bog the system


Different approaches and technologies emerged to support each à                           Online Analytical Processing
(supporting data warehouses) and Online Transaction Processing
































https://ashutosh-bitmesra.medium.com/oltp-and-olap-what-are-the-differences-a6e21f25bfe0

     What   ar e  dat a  war ehouses?


     A logical collection of information –        gathered from many
     different operational databases –          that supports business
     analysis activities and decision-making tasks.


     The primary purpose of a data warehouse is to aggregate
     information throughout an organisation into a single
     re p os i t or y   f or   d e ci s i on-making purposes.


     The data warehouse provided the ability to support
     decision making without disrupting day-to-day operations


     Historical data, doesn't change! Can then be used to do
     Business Intelligence Analysis.






                                                                                                                                                   33


Kroenke, Bunker & Wilson 2014, p. 289

What   ar e  dat a  mar t s,   dat a  mi ni ng,   dat a  l akes?


Data mart
contains a subset of
data warehouse information


Data-mining
A process in which algorithms are applied
to information to uncover patterns and
re l at i ons hi p s   ot he r w i s e   d i f f i cul t   t o  f i nd .



























Baltzan, Lynch & Fisher 2015, p. 279
                                                                                                                                                                            34

What   i s  t he  di ffer ence  bet ween  busi ness  i nt el l i gence
and business analytics?


The two terms are used interchangeably in many contexts –              but, one distinction is that BI often
has an historical   focus, whilst analytics has a predictive      focus







             Explore historical                                                                      Predictive
                           data                                                                      capabilities
